intro: The clearest difference between SonarQube and StackHawk is pricing — SonarQube is freemium (has a free tier), while StackHawk is subscription (no free tier). Beyond that, lock-in — SonarQube has low lock-in (OSS Community edition available), whereas StackHawk has low lock-in (Based on OSS ZAP scanner). The stated fit for both tools is similar on paper: SonarQube is pitched as SAST and code quality platform detecting security vulnerabilities, bugs, and code smells across 30+ programming languages, while StackHawk is pitched as Developer-centric DAST tool running application security tests in CI/CD using OpenAPI and GraphQL specs. On the strengths side, SonarQube is cited for UGC platforms (social, marketplace), while StackHawk is cited for Chat/messaging apps. The honest trade-off: both SonarQube and StackHawk are the wrong call for internal projects. Choosing between them should come down to the constraint that actually binds your workflow, not surface-level overlap. On developer experience the data shows SonarQube at 5/5 and StackHawk at 5/5, with transparency at 4/5 and 4/5 respectively.
quick_take: SonarQube and StackHawk both target Content Moderation API; their data-level fit descriptions overlap, so decide based on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose SonarQube if you need a Content Moderation API tool and a freemium (has a free tier) pricing shape fits your budget better than subscription (no free tier).
verdict_b: Choose StackHawk if you need a Content Moderation API tool and a subscription (no free tier) pricing shape fits your budget better than freemium (has a free tier).

faq.a: SonarQube is freemium with a free tier, while StackHawk is subscription, so the cheaper option depends on your usage shape. Listed pricing pages usually change, so check both directly before committing. For small teams, the free-tier limits often matter more than headline prices.

faq.q: Can I migrate from SonarQube to StackHawk?
faq.a: Migrating from SonarQube to StackHawk is feasible when their scopes overlap, but the work is in re-mapping configuration, data models, and integrations. SonarQube has low lock-in, so exporting state is typically straightforward. Plan for a dual-run period so you can validate parity before decommissioning.

SonarQube and StackHawk score the same on developer experience (5/5 each) in our data. Choose based on which workflow, docs style, and CLI ergonomics feel closer to your team's preferences. Trial both in a small project before committing.

faq.q: Is StackHawk a good alternative to SonarQube?
faq.a: StackHawk is a reasonable alternative to SonarQube when StackHawk fits marketplace, apps. If your use case matches SonarQube's sweet spot — marketplace, apps — staying with SonarQube is probably safer. SonarQube itself is not meant for internal projects, which is often where users start shopping for alternatives.
